g.data-narration: Welcome to File System Implementation - the foundation of how operating systems organize and manage data on disk storage.
A file system translates user-friendly file operations into physical storage operations, organizing data into hierarchical structures.
g.text: File system implementation refers to the methods and data structures an OS uses to
organize, store, and retrieve files on storage devices. Users see logical file
operations, but the OS must translate these into physical disk storage.

g.data-narration: File systems are organized in layers - from user commands down to physical disk hardware. Each layer handles specific responsibilities.
A disk is partitioned into sections - boot code, superblock with metadata, free space tracking, i-nodes, and actual data blocks.
Contiguous allocation stores each file in consecutive disk blocks. Fast for sequential access but suffers from external fragmentation.
g.text: Each file occupies a set of consecutive blocks. Directory stores starting block + length.

g.data-narration: Linked allocation treats each block as part of a linked list. Each block contains a pointer to the next, allowing non-contiguous storage.
g.text: Each block contains a pointer to the next block. Directory stores first and last block pointers.

g.data-narration: Indexed allocation uses a separate index block storing pointers to all data blocks. Supports random access efficiently without fragmentation.
g.text: All pointers collected in an index block. Directory stores index block address.

g.data-narration: UNIX uses i-nodes with 12 direct pointers, plus single, double, and triple indirect pointers to support very large files efficiently.
g.text: 12 direct pointers + single/double/triple indirect pointers for large file support.

g.data-narration: Bit vectors track free blocks using one bit per block - simple and efficient for finding contiguous free space.
g.text: Each block represented by a bit. 0 = free, 1 = allocated. Easy to find contiguous blocks.

g.data-narration: Linked list method connects free blocks together via pointers. Simple but inefficient for finding multiple contiguous blocks.
g.text: Free blocks linked together. OS maintains pointer to first free block.

g.data-narration: Grouping stores addresses of multiple free blocks in one block. Counting stores ranges of contiguous free blocks.
g.text: More Free Space Methods: Grouping & Counting
Grouping: Quick allocation of multiple blocks

g.data-narration: Directories map filenames to file metadata. Linear lists are simple but slow; hash tables provide O(1) lookup but handle collisions.
File systems use buffer caches to store frequently accessed blocks. Replacement algorithms like LRU determine which blocks to evict.
